content:
# The Last Good Clock

Margaret kept the kitchen in a particular order. Spoons nested in the drawer by size. The dish towel folded in thirds, never halves. A small ceramic rooster on the windowsill, always facing east.

She noticed the rooster had turned sometime in February. Not dramatically — just a few degrees, enough that its red comb no longer caught the morning light the way it used to. She turned it back. A week later, it had shifted again.

The drawer was worse. She found a fork among the spoons, a butter knife sideways across everything. She hadn't put it there. She was certain.

Her daughter visited in March and said the word Margaret had been avoiding: *help*. Someone to come in. A few hours a week.

Margaret stood at the window after her daughter left and looked at the rooster. She picked it up. The paint on its base had worn thin in a small oval where her thumb always rested. She hadn't noticed that before — how her own grip had contributed to the wearing down of things.

She set it back, facing east.

The spoons would migrate again. The towel would unfold itself in the night, or seem to. The house would continue its slow, patient work of coming undone, the way all houses do, the way all things do, given enough time and enough living inside them.

She put the kettle on.
